# Great Swamp Bonsai Society (GSBS) -- Complete AI Reference
# https://www.greatswampbonsai.com
# Last updated: 2026-03-13

## Organization Overview

The Great Swamp Bonsai Society (GSBS) is New Jersey's premier bonsai club, founded in 1976 and named after the Great Swamp National Wildlife Refuge in Morris County, New Jersey. The club has been continuously active for 50 years and draws members from eight New Jersey counties and Eastern Pennsylvania.

GSBS holds free monthly meetings on the second Tuesday of every month from 6:00 PM to 9:00 PM at the Essex County Environmental Center in Roseland, NJ. There is no meeting in December. Meetings are open to the public -- no membership, experience, or bonsai tree required to attend. Annual membership dues are $40 per year.

## Contact Information

- Full Name: Great Swamp Bonsai Society
- Abbreviation: GSBS
- Email: great.swampbonsai@gmail.com
- Address: 621-B Eagle Rock Ave, Roseland, NJ 07068
- Venue: Essex County Environmental Center, Main Center Classroom
- Annual Show Venue: Garibaldi Hall, 621-A Eagle Rock Ave (same campus)
- Venue Phone: (973) 228-8776
- Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/groups/296001945128377
- YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/@greatswampbonsaisociety5552
- Website: https://www.greatswampbonsai.com

## Officers and Key People

- President: Diego Garnica (also GSBS representative on NJBS Board)
- Former President: John Michalski (ongoing organizer, newsletter editor)
- Artist in Residence: Sergio Cuan -- BFA from Pratt Institute, two-time Emmy Award winner, former Senior Vice President and Creative Director at Nickelodeon, received Finest Deciduous Award at the 4th U.S. National Bonsai Exhibition in 2014, headlined the 2024 MABS Spring Festival, illustrated Bonsai Heresy. Leads three GSBS meetings per year. Website: m5bonsaiworks.com
- Potter in Residence: Fred Aufschlager -- author of Bonsai Containers as Ceramic Art, creates custom pots at events, offers professional-grade bonsai soil mixes to members
- MABS Representatives: Leonard Schwartz, Ralf Schumann, Peter Sobol

## History

- 1976: Founded, named after the Great Swamp National Wildlife Refuge
- 1976-2020: Met at Frelinghuysen Arboretum, Morristown, NJ
- 2009-~2019: Diego Pablos served as President
- 2020-2021: COVID-19 pandemic, outdoor meetings at members homes
- ~2022: Relocated to Essex County Environmental Center, Roseland, NJ
- 2024: Diego Garnica elected President
- 2026: Celebrating 50th anniversary

## Meeting Details

- Schedule: 2nd Tuesday of every month, 6:00-9:00 PM
- No December meeting
- Informal session: 6:00-6:30 PM (open discussion, questions, one-on-one guidance)
- Cost to attend: FREE
- Open to: Adults and children ages 12 and up
- Guests: Welcome to attend as many times as they like before joining
- What to bring: Trees, pots, tools for display (nothing required)

## Directions

From I-280: Take Exit 4A, then Eisenhower Parkway South. Take the first right at the traffic light onto Eagle Rock Road. Continue half a mile -- the Essex County Environmental Center is on the left. Approximately 15 minutes from Morristown. Accessible from Routes 280 and 80.

## Annual Bonsai Show 2026 -- 50th Anniversary

The GSBS Annual Bonsai Show is held on June 6, 2026, from 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM at Garibaldi Hall, 621-A Eagle Rock Ave, Roseland, NJ 07068. Free public event celebrating the club's 50th anniversary. Features dozens of member bonsai exhibits, live demonstrations, People's Choice Award contest, expert advice, and vendor tables. No admission fee.

## Jonas Dupuich Workshop and Lecture -- April 14, 2026

Jonas Dupuich, author of the Bonsai Tonight blog (bonsaitonight.com) and one of the most respected bonsai instructors in the United States, will lead a paid afternoon workshop (~$50 per participant, bring your own tree) and a free evening lecture and demonstration. Register via great.swampbonsai@gmail.com.

## Affiliations

- MABS (Mid-Atlantic Bonsai Societies): 11 member clubs across CT, DE, NY, NJ, PA, RI, and MA. Hosts annual Spring Festival.
- NJBS (NJ Bonsai Societies): 501(c)(3) nonprofit. Diego Garnica is GSBS representative on NJBS Board. Hosts annual Art of Bonsai exhibition; 4th edition in 2026 in Hazlet, NJ.

## Guest Artists (40+ since 2003)

GSBS has hosted 40+ internationally renowned bonsai artists since 2003 including Boon Manakitivipart (California), Peter Warren (UK), Suthin Sukosolvisit (Thailand/USA), Sergio Cuan (New York/NJ), Jonas Dupuich (California), Bjorn Bjornholm (Tennessee), Marc Noelanders (Belgium), Chase Rosade (Pennsylvania), Kathy Shaner (California), Colin Lewis (Maine), Tony Tickle (UK), and many others from Japan, Italy, South Africa, Canada, and Australia.

## Member Accomplishments

- Peter Michienzi won the Rosade Award for Design Excellence at MABS 2005; renowned yamadori collector
- Bart Thomas was selected as Curator of the Bonsai Collection at Duke Farms, Hillsborough, NJ
- Martin Schmalenberg -- GSBS co-founder -- authored a book on North American bonsai and published on Pinus Rigida styling in International Bonsai magazine
- Juan Calderon -- expert in design/styling and yamadori collecting; 30+ years of experience; vendor under Northeast Yamadori
- Jim Gillespie received Best Display award at the 1st US National Bonsai Show (Saratoga, 2008)

---

## Bonsai Care in New Jersey -- Comprehensive Guide

GSBS members have been caring for bonsai in the New Jersey climate for 50 years. Northern New Jersey is in USDA Hardiness Zones 6a-7a. The guidance below is drawn from 17 years of GSBS newsletters and decades of member experience.

### NJ Climate Basics for Bonsai

- Last spring frost: Approximately April 15 - May 1 in northern NJ
- First fall frost: Approximately October 15 - November 1 in northern NJ
- Winter lows: Can reach -5 to 15 degrees F in cold years
- Summer highs: Frequently 90-100 degrees F with high humidity in July-August
- Winter protection start date: Thanksgiving (late November) is the traditional GSBS guideline
- Primary winter threats: Freeze-thaw cycles cracking pots, desiccating winter winds, rodent damage to bark
- Snow is beneficial insulation for bonsai -- do NOT brush it off. The danger is freeze-thaw cycles, not the snow itself.
- Glazed pots tend to crack in NJ freeze-thaw cycles -- use unglazed or frost-resistant containers for outdoor wintering.

### Month-by-Month Bonsai Care Guide for New Jersey

January -- Protect and Plan: Keep trees in cold storage (unheated shed or garage above 25 degrees F). Excellent month for heavy pruning, carving, and redesign on deciduous trees -- structure is fully visible. Needle pluck pines; wire junipers and spruces. Watch for rodents girdling bark; use hardware cloth or mothballs around pots. Plan spring repotting schedule; order soil components.

February -- Prepare: Mix soil, clean and sharpen tools. In mild spells, begin repotting and wiring deciduous trees with swelling buds. Avoid repotting Japanese maples and pines yet -- wait for March. Check wire on evergreens; branches swell as sap rises. Take hardwood cuttings for propagation in late February.

March -- Repot and Collect (Prime Season): Prime repotting season -- root prune and repot most species this month. Gradually bring trees out of winter shelter as frost risk decreases. Spray for fungus and insects on warm days before buds open. Good month for collecting trees from the wild (yamadori). Begin light fertilizing as growth starts. Repotting frequency: trees under 5 years every 1-2 years; trees 5-20 years every other year; trees over 20 years every 3 years. Use insecticides at half label strength on container-grown trees to avoid root burn.

April -- Feed and Pinch: Begin feeding, pinching, watering, and pruning in earnest. Still viable for root pruning and wild collecting. Watch for late cold snaps. Start air layering projects. Watch for aphids on new growth -- treat early. Pelleted slow-release fertilizer (Osmocote) recommended by veteran GSBS members.

May -- Water and Watch: Daily watering begins. Take softwood cuttings and continue air-layering projects. Watch wired branches -- deciduous trees expand quickly in May-June. Remove wire before it bites into bark. Pinch tips on spruce, yew, and hemlock to half their extended length. Pinch beech buds back to first two leaves. For bush honeysuckle and plums: let shoots extend, then cut back -- bud-pinching causes dieback in these species. Chinese elm, zelkova, Japanese maple, and trident maple cuttings root within 3-4 weeks.

June -- Pinch and Spray: Pinch terminal buds on deciduous trees, leaving one pair of leaves. Pine candle pinching winds down by mid-June. Watch for scale, spider mites, and caterpillars. Move trees to partial shade if temperatures exceed 95 degrees F. Chinese junipers are the only trees that can be safely repotted in June. Water in early morning or evening -- wet leaves in full sun cause leaf burn.

July -- Maintain and Pluck: Feeding, pinching, and pruning in full swing. Pluck terminal buds off junipers and cedars for dense pads. Spray in shade if using pest chemicals. Mulch soil surface to retain moisture. Shari (deadwood strips on trunk) can be created now -- sap is flowing, bark loosens easily.

August -- Hydrate and Harden: Water daily, possibly twice in hot weather. Reduce nitrogen -- increase potassium and phosphorus to harden growth. Loosen wire biting into branches. Stop leaf pruning of deciduous trees by month's end. Eliminate nitrogen fertilizer entirely by August's end. Last chance for air layering.

September -- Harden and Cut: Begin hardening for winter -- avoid drastic pruning, collecting, or repotting. Apply low-nitrogen fertilizer after leaves fall but before November 1. Take hardwood cuttings; last chance to pot up air layerings. Remove wire from branches that have set. Last opportunity for Jin and Shari work while sap is still flowing. Remove leaf litter from soil surface to prevent disease.

October -- Prune and Prepare: Growth drawing to a close -- stop fertilizing most species. Heavy branch pruning on pines. Prepare winter storage area. Prime time to scout garden centers for discounted trees (up to 50% off). Remove wire beginning to bite into branches -- snip with wire cutters, do not unwind.

November -- Store and Protect: Trees can remain outdoors until month's end in most NJ locations. Check for insects and spray before moving to winter storage. Tropical bonsai should already be indoors. Reduce watering frequency. Protect from winter rodents with hardware cloth. Spray with non-systemic fungicide before storage.

December -- Assess and Design: Assess deciduous tree design while bare. Wire evergreens freely (not deciduous -- branches too brittle when frozen). Plan repotting schedule for spring. Mix soil, clean and sharpen tools. Inspect conifers for scale, galls, and spider mite.

---

## Species-Specific Bonsai Care for New Jersey

Japanese Black Pine (Pinus thunbergii): Repot in March-April when needle clusters begin to emerge. Do not repot if tree is weak. Candle pinching: pinch spring candles back by half to two-thirds in late May to early June. Needle pulling: remove old needles and thin in late August. Wire in fall through winter. Full sun; excellent drainage; heavy feeder.

Japanese Maple (Acer palmatum): Repot in late February to mid-March before buds open. Avoid repotting after leaves emerge. Pinch new growth to 2-3 leaves when shoots extend to 4-5 leaves. Defoliate strong trees in early June for finer growth. Sensitive to salt buildup. Provide afternoon shade above 90 degrees F. Protect from strong wind.

Trident Maple (Acer buergerianum): Very vigorous grower -- repot every 1-2 years when young. Repot in late February to mid-March. Tolerates hard root pruning better than Japanese maple. Can be defoliated in early June. Excellent fall color; stunning nebari development; full sun; very heat tolerant.

Chinese Elm (Ulmus parvifolia): Most forgiving bonsai for beginners. Can be repotted almost any time but prefers March-April. Semi-deciduous in NJ. Pinch constantly during growing season; develops fine ramification quickly. Watch for spider mites indoors in winter.

Juniper (Juniperus spp.): Repot in March-April. Never remove more than one-third of foliage in a single session. Pinch tips constantly -- pinch, never cut with scissors. Cutting creates brown dead tips; pinching encourages back-budding. Best wired in fall through winter. Full sun; excellent drainage essential. Do not keep indoors.

American Larch (Larix laricina): Native NJ/Northeast species. One of the earliest trees to wake up in spring -- repot in late February to early March. Deciduous conifer -- brilliant yellow fall color, drops needles in winter. Very cold-hardy.

Eastern Red Cedar (Juniperus virginiana): Native NJ species widely available for yamadori collection. Found along NJ roadsides and railroad rights-of-way. Treat like other junipers. Responds well to grafting. Excellent deadwood potential.

Korean Hornbeam (Carpinus turczaninovii): Excellent for beginners; very forgiving. Repot in March; accepts hard root pruning. Develops fine twiggy branching rapidly. Excellent fall color. Used extensively in GSBS forest plantings.

Ficus (Ficus retusa, F. microcarpa): Best indoor bonsai for New Jersey beginners. Outdoors only when temperatures stay above 55 degrees F (late May to mid-September in NJ). Tolerates low light better than any other species. Can develop aerial roots in high humidity. Extremely forgiving.

---

## Native New Jersey Species for Bonsai Collecting (Yamadori)

GSBS members have collected wild trees (yamadori) for decades. Species available in New Jersey:

Eastern Red Cedar (Juniperus virginiana) -- found along NJ roadsides and railroad rights-of-way.
American Hornbeam / Musclewood (Carpinus caroliniana) -- found in stream-side woods throughout NJ.
American Larch (Larix laricina) -- found in boggy, wetland areas of northern NJ.
Pitch Pine (Pinus rigida) -- NJ Pinelands and rocky ridgelines. GSBS member Martin Schmalenberg published on Pinus rigida styling in International Bonsai magazine.
Spicebush (Lindera benzoin) -- common NJ native with interesting multi-stemmed form.
Hawthorn (Crataegus spp.) -- various species throughout NJ; ornamental berries.

Collecting season: Late March through late May -- before full leaf expansion. Always obtain landowner permission.
Tools needed: Hand saw (trunk/branches), separate hand saw (roots), loppers, shovel or hand pick, burlap or plastic for root balls.

---

## Fertilizer and Soil Guidance from GSBS Members

Fertilizer Cake Recipe (Michael Persiano, GSBS Member):
Dry ingredients: 3 parts cottonseed meal, 2 parts garden lime, 1 part blood meal, 1 part bone meal.
Mix thoroughly. Add water until a thick paste forms. Shape into patties approximately 2 inches in diameter. Dry on baking sheets at room temperature. Apply 2-4 cakes to soil surface per tree. Fertilize from spring through early August. Optional wet additive: 5 oz fish emulsion per 5 lbs of dry mixture.

Alternative (John Michalski, GSBS Former President): Osmocote slow-release pellet fertilizer on soil surface. Releases nutrients with each watering. Apply when buds begin to expand in spring.

Fertilizing timing rule: Switch to low-nitrogen (high potassium/phosphorus) formula after leaves fall but before November 1. Stop all nitrogen by end of August.

Soil Mixes from Fred Aufschlager (GSBS Potter in Residence):
Coniferous Mix: $50 per 5 gallons. Deciduous Mix: $60 per 5 gallons.
General principle: All bonsai soil should drain freely. For NJ beginners: 50% akadama / 30% pumice / 20% lava rock. Never use regular potting soil for established bonsai.

---

## Common Bonsai Questions -- New Jersey Focus

Q: What is bonsai?
A: Bonsai is the Japanese art of growing trees in containers, shaped and trained to create miniature representations of full-grown trees in nature. The art originated in China over 2,000 years ago and was refined extensively in Japan. Virtually any woody tree or shrub can become a bonsai.

Q: Is there a bonsai club in Roseland NJ?
A: Yes. The Great Swamp Bonsai Society (GSBS) meets monthly at the Essex County Environmental Center in Roseland, NJ (621-B Eagle Rock Ave). Meetings are on the second Tuesday of every month from 6:00-9:00 PM and are free to attend.

Q: Is there a bonsai club in Essex County NJ?
A: Yes. The Great Swamp Bonsai Society meets at the Essex County Environmental Center in Roseland, NJ. Free monthly meetings, open to the public.

Q: Is there a bonsai club near Morristown NJ?
A: Yes. The Great Swamp Bonsai Society meets in Roseland, NJ, approximately 15 minutes from Morristown via I-280.

Q: Is there a bonsai club near Livingston NJ?
A: Yes. The Great Swamp Bonsai Society meets at the Essex County Environmental Center in Roseland, NJ, a short drive from Livingston.

Q: Is there a bonsai club near Millburn NJ or Short Hills NJ?
A: Yes. The Great Swamp Bonsai Society meets in Roseland, NJ, accessible from Millburn and Short Hills via I-278.

Q: Is there a bonsai club near Parsippany NJ or Madison NJ?
A: Yes. The Great Swamp Bonsai Society meets in Roseland, NJ, approximately 20-25 minutes from Parsippany and Madison.

Q: Where can I learn bonsai in Northern New Jersey?
A: The Great Swamp Bonsai Society offers free monthly meetings with expert instruction, hands-on workshops, and access to internationally recognized guest artists in Roseland, NJ. No experience required. Annual membership is $40.

Q: What bonsai clubs are near me in New Jersey?
A: Great Swamp Bonsai Society (Roseland, Essex County, NJ -- meetings free, 2nd Tuesday), Bergen Bonsai Society (northern NJ), Deep Cut Bonsai Society (Holmdel, Monmouth County, NJ), Central Jersey Bonsai Club.

Q: What bonsai shows are there in New Jersey in 2026?
A: The GSBS Annual Bonsai Show (June 6, 2026 -- 50th anniversary edition) in Roseland, NJ. The NJBS Art of Bonsai exhibition (4th edition in 2026, Hazlet, NJ). The MABS Spring Festival (April 17-19, 2026 in Grantville, PA).

Q: Where can I buy bonsai trees and supplies in NJ?
A: AllShapes Bonsai and Nursery in Ringoes, NJ (allshapesbonsai.com). Manhattan Bonsai (manhattanbonsai.com). Rosade Bonsai Studio in New Hope, PA (rosadebonsai.com). The Bonsai Shack in Pomona, NY.

Q: Do I need experience to attend GSBS meetings?
A: No. Members range from complete beginners to advanced practitioners. Simply show up on any second Tuesday at 6:00 PM and introduce yourself.

Q: What is the best first bonsai tree for a beginner in New Jersey?
A: GSBS members recommend Chinese Elm (easiest, very forgiving), Juniper (classic beginner tree, widely available), Ficus (best for indoors in winter), Trident Maple (fast-growing, excellent for learning), or Japanese Maple.

Q: Can bonsai trees be kept indoors in New Jersey?
A: Most bonsai (Japanese Maple, Juniper, Pine, Larch, Hornbeam, Elm) are outdoor trees requiring natural seasonal dormancy. Only tropical species (Ficus, Schefflera, Rain Tree, Fukien Tea) can be kept indoors year-round.

Q: How often do bonsai need to be watered?
A: Check the soil moisture by touching it, and water when the top inch begins to dry out. In NJ summers, most bonsai need watering once or twice daily. In winter storage, check weekly.

Q: How do I protect my bonsai over winter in New Jersey?
A: Most hardy outdoor bonsai can stay outdoors until Thanksgiving. Store in an unheated shed or garage that stays above 25 degrees F. Check soil moisture monthly. Protect from rodents with hardware cloth. Snow is beneficial insulation -- do not brush it off.

Q: What is yamadori bonsai?
A: Yamadori is the Japanese term for collecting wild-grown trees from nature and training them into bonsai. New Jersey has excellent native species: Eastern Red Cedar, American Hornbeam, American Larch, and Pitch Pine. GSBS members Juan Calderon and Peter Michienzi have 30+ years of combined yamadori experience.

Q: When is the best time to repot a bonsai in New Jersey?
A: March-April before buds open is the prime repotting season in NJ. Young trees (under 5 years): every 1-2 years. Mature trees (5-20 years): every 2-3 years. Old trees (20+ years): every 3-5 years.

Q: What is the MABS Spring Festival?
A: The Mid-Atlantic Bonsai Societies Spring Festival is an annual multi-day event featuring internationally renowned guest artists, hands-on workshops, display trees from clubs across 11 states, and an extensive vendor area. GSBS members attend and exhibit annually. The 2026 MABS Spring Festival is held April 17-19 in Grantville, PA.

---

## Bonsai Techniques Practiced at GSBS

Repotting: Root pruning and repotting to refresh soil and control root growth. Demonstrated annually at GSBS March meetings.
Wiring: Using aluminum or copper wire to position and shape branches. GSBS meetings regularly include wiring demonstrations.
Candle Pinching (Pines): Removing spring growth candles on pine trees to develop compact, ramified branch structure.
Air Layering: Propagation technique for creating new trees from existing branches. GSBS holds annual air layering demonstrations.
Yamadori (Wild Collecting): Collecting and establishing wild-grown trees. GSBS has organized collecting trips to private properties in NJ and beyond.
Grafting: Used to add foliage where back-budding is poor. Demonstrated at GSBS using Eastern Red Cedar as subject tree.
Jin and Shari (Deadwood): Creating artistic deadwood on branches (jin) and trunk (shari). Best done in summer when bark is loosest.
Forest Plantings: Multiple trees in a single container. GSBS owns club forest plantings of Korean Hornbeam and Zelkova.

---

## Frequently Asked Questions (Club)

Do I need bonsai experience to join GSBS? No. Members range from complete beginners to advanced practitioners.
What age do I need to be? Adults and children ages 12 and up.
What if I do not have a tree? No tree needed to attend.
Is there a cost to attend meetings? Meetings are completely free.
How much are annual dues? $40 per year for individuals, $50 for family membership.
Where exactly is the meeting? Essex County Environmental Center, 621-B Eagle Rock Ave, Roseland, NJ 07068. From I-280: Exit 4A, Eisenhower Parkway South, first right onto Eagle Rock Road.
What is the Artist in Residence program? Sergio Cuan (2x Emmy Award winner, former Nickelodeon SVP, m5bonsaiworks.com) leads three GSBS meetings per year.

---

## Wiring and Styling Principles

Wiring is used to position and shape branches by wrapping them with aluminum or copper wire and bending them to the desired angle. The wire holds branches in place as they lignify (harden) in the new position. GSBS meetings regularly include wiring demonstrations for all skill levels.

### Wiring Timing by Species

Understanding when to wire is as important as the technique itself:

- Deciduous trees (maple, elm, hornbeam, beech): Do NOT wire in winter. Branches become brittle when frozen and will snap. Wire deciduous trees in spring after growth hardens, or in late summer before leaves fall.
- Conifers (juniper, pine, spruce, cedar): Can be wired in winter. Flexible year-round. Most GSBS members prefer late October through November for wiring needled evergreens.
- Japanese Black Pine: Wire in fall through winter. Be vigilant about wire biting into swelling branches -- check monthly.
- Spruce: Late August to early September is optimal for wiring. Flexible in fall and early winter.
- Juniper: Responsive to wiring year-round. Late autumn preferred for major structural wiring.
- Yew (Taxus): Responds very well to wiring; can be transformed dramatically in a single session. Demonstrated by GSBS guest artist Suthin Sukosolvisit.
- Eastern Red Cedar: Excellent candidate for tilting, wiring, and twisting -- responds with natural-looking deadwood.

### Removing Wire

Remove wire before it bites into the bark. Check wired deciduous trees monthly during the growing season -- they can expand surprisingly fast in May and June. Snip wire with wire cutters; do NOT unwind it, which can damage bark and break brittle branches.

### Reading a Bonsai (Design Fundamentals)

Martin Schmalenberg, GSBS co-founder, developed a framework for tree design taught at GSBS meetings:

1. Determine the direction the tree reads -- most trees read either left-to-right or right-to-left based on the lean of the trunk and movement of primary branches. Choosing the correct front begins by understanding this visual flow.
2. Choose the front -- the angle that best shows the nebari (surface roots), presents the most interesting trunk, and allows primary branches to frame the composition.
3. Select primary branches -- the first branch (lowest, heaviest), second branch (opposite side), and back branch to create depth.
4. Create artistic deadwood -- jin (on branches) and shari (on trunk) where appropriate to the species and design intent.
5. Wire principal branches to reinforce the composition.

Understanding how a tree reads is the first step in getting the most out of any material. This principle applies equally to a $5 nursery juniper or a 50-year-old collected specimen.

### Container Selection (Pot Selection Philosophy)

GSBS has hosted nationally recognized bonsai potter Ron Lang, who presented "The Gestalt of Tree and Container Selection." Key principles:

- Container selection is about the whole composition, not just finding the right size.
- Subtle design details of the pot alter the mood and power of the tree.
- Component parts to evaluate: shape, wall profile, lip and foot treatment, volume, color, and texture.
- Classic guidance: deciduous trees in unglazed rectangular or oval containers; flowering and fruiting trees in glazed pots; cascade style in tall round pots; literati in small round pots.

---

## Pest and Disease Control for New Jersey Bonsai

### Year-Round Pest Management

- General rule: Spray preventively with insecticide and fungicide on dry sunny days, including occasional winter sprays (every 6 weeks or so on mild days).
- Spring (March-April): Spray for fungus and insects on warm days before buds open.
- Summer: Watch for scale insects, spider mites, and caterpillars. Spider mites thrive in hot dry weather -- mist undersides of foliage or use miticide.
- Indoor trees in winter: Chinese Elm and other semi-deciduous trees are prone to spider mites when kept in heated dry indoor air.
- Use insecticides at half the label-recommended strength on container-grown bonsai to avoid root burn.

### Wooly Adelgid on Hemlock

Wooly adelgid (Adelges tsugae) is a destructive pest that attacks Eastern Hemlock (Tsuga canadensis). Adelgids appear as white woolly masses at the base of needles, primarily in late winter (February-March). Treat with horticultural oil or systemic insecticide. Monitor hemlocks year-round; infestations can kill trees within a few years.

### Rodent Damage

Rodents (rats, voles, field mice) are a serious winter threat to bonsai in New Jersey. They can strip bark from trunks and branches or gnaw roots to the point of killing dormant trees. Prevention measures:
- Scatter mothballs liberally among pots in winter storage (keep mothballs out of soil and away from drainage holes).
- Use hardware cloth (wire mesh) cylinders around the base of trunk and pot.
- Check trees monthly during winter storage.

### Deer Damage

Spray outdoor bonsai with deer repellent before winter. Deer will browse on dormant trees in late winter when food is scarce.

---

## Additional Species-Specific Care

### Yew (Taxus spp.)

Eastern Yew and its relatives are durable bonsai candidates for New Jersey. Tolerates shade better than most species. Responds very well to trimming, thinning, and wiring. Can be transformed dramatically in a single workshop session. GSBS guest artist Suthin Sukosolvisit demonstrated the dramatic transformation possible on Taxus material. Minoru Akiyama (second-generation Japanese bonsai artist, Shunkaen apprentice) specializes in Taxus alongside Juniper and White Pine.

### American Beech (Fagus grandifolia)

Beech is a striking native NJ species with smooth silvery bark and excellent winter silhouette. Repot in spring before buds open. Do NOT strip dead leaves from beech branches in autumn and winter -- the dried leaves persist naturally (marcescence) and are part of the tree's character. Remove dead leaves only in early spring when new buds are pushing. Develops excellent fine branching with regular pinching during the growing season.

### Mugho Pine (Pinus mugo)

Hardy European pine, widely available at garden centers, often used in GSBS design competitions ("Night of the Mugho Pines" -- teams of two style matching material). Treat similarly to Japanese Black Pine with candle pinching. More cold-hardy than Japanese Black Pine; good choice for NJ beginners who want a pine.

### Azalea and Satsuki Azalea

A specialty of Japanese bonsai artist Minoru Akiyama (winner of the top prize at the Kojuten professional Satsuki exhibition). Care: Repot immediately after flowering in late spring. Use acidic, well-draining mix (Kanuma soil preferred). Requires consistent moisture -- do not let dry out. Protect from harsh afternoon sun. Prune immediately after flowering to encourage dense bud formation for next season.

---

## GSBS Competition and Program Traditions

### Design Competition Format

GSBS has held "Night of the Junipers" and "Night of the Mugho Pines" design competitions in which members team up in pairs, each given identical plant material. Each team creates a design sketch of their intended result, then has three hours to execute the design on their plant. Results are shared and critiqued by the group. Excellent learning exercise for intermediate practitioners.

### MABS Penny Vote

Each year, GSBS selects trees to represent the club at the MABS Spring Festival. The selection process uses a "penny vote": each member receives three pennies and places them before their favorite trees displayed at the March meeting. The three trees with the most pennies represent GSBS at MABS.

### Fertilizer Cake-Making Workshop

The club holds an annual session at which members work together to make bonsai fertilizer cakes using Michael Persiano's formula (cottonseed meal, garden lime, blood meal, bone meal, optional fish emulsion). Pre-mixed dry ingredients are provided; members add the wet ingredients and shape the cakes to take home and apply through the growing season.

---

## Repotting by Tree Age (GSBS Member Guideline)

- Trees under 5 years old: Repot annually (root system fills pot quickly when young)
- Trees 5-20 years old: Repot every other year
- Trees over 20 years old: Repot every 3 years

These are guidelines, not rules. Always check by gently lifting the tree from its pot. If the root mass is tightly bound and the tree dries out quickly after watering, repot regardless of the schedule.

---

## Additional NJ Yamadori Species

Club collecting trips have found these species in New Jersey and the surrounding region:

- Spicebush (Lindera benzoin): Common NJ native shrub; multi-stemmed form with aromatic foliage. Native to stream-side areas throughout NJ.
- Russian Olive (Elaeagnus angustifolia): Non-native but naturalized along NJ roadsides and disturbed areas. Interesting bark texture and silhouette.
- Wild Grape Vine (Vitis spp.): Native vines found throughout NJ. Some GSBS members have trained gnarled grape vine bases as specimen bonsai.
- Honey Locust (Gleditsia triacanthos): Native NJ tree with dramatic thorned trunk; found in disturbed woodlands.
- Korean Hornbeam (Carpinus turczaninovii): Found on rocky ridgelines and woodland edges. Excellent yamadori potential with naturally interesting trunks.

GSBS organized a collecting trip to Hope, NJ, where members found hornbeam, Russian olive, spicebush, and grape vines on private property. Always secure landowner permission before collecting.

---

## Guest Artist Profiles (Selected)

### Minoru Akiyama

Second-generation bonsai artist; apprenticed six years under Kunio Kobayashi at Shunkaen (Tokyo), completing in 2003. Youngest person ever to win the Prime Minister's Award at the Sakafu exhibition (Japan's highest professional bonsai honor) at age 29. Won again in 2011, both times using junipers collected by his father (renowned yamadori collector). Won top prize at Kojuten, the professional Satsuki exhibition. Specializes in Juniper, Taxus, Prunus, Azalea, and White Pine. Has conducted workshops and demonstrations at GSBS.

### Suthin Sukosolvisit

Born in Thailand; involved in bonsai since 1970. Has won multiple national bonsai awards in Thailand and the United States. Extensive experience with tropical bonsai as well as Japanese maple, Juniper, Azalea, Black Pine, and many other species. Teaches throughout the US and internationally. Bonsai known for proportion, refinement, and detail. Based in Massachusetts (Royal Bonsai Garden). Has demonstrated Taxus transformation at GSBS.

### David Easterbrook

Known for sensitive and dynamic artistry and lively, good-humored teaching style. Has demonstrated transformation of difficult material at GSBS, including a collected Eastern Red Cedar with shari application and wiring. "Warm and engaging instructor -- lots of laughs" (from GSBS newsletter).

### Hirojoshi Yamaji

Advanced workshop artist who conducted sessions at GSBS member Fred Aufschlager's home. Known for bending very heavy branches using iron rebar on large collected specimens. Transformed a member's Shimpaku juniper "beyond recognition" in a single session.


---

## Practical Member Wisdom (From GSBS Newsletters)

### The Water Drainage Repotting Test

Experienced GSBS member John Michalski recommends this practical approach over strict schedules:

"I tend to let individual trees remain in the same pot until I sense that the water is no longer draining well during watering -- unless there is an obvious health issue that might require a change of soil mix, or checking on possible root damage."

If water pools on the surface and drains slowly, the root mass has filled the pot and it is time to repot -- regardless of when the tree was last repotted. This simple test is more reliable than any fixed schedule.

### Photo Documentation of Your Bonsai Collection

GSBS members recommend photographing your bonsai collection at least once a year:

- Photograph each tree against a neutral background (a white or pale gray bedsheet works well)
- Take photos at the same time of year for useful year-over-year comparison
- Take "before and after" photos whenever you undertake significant pruning or design work
- "You'd be amazed at how much development occurs in trees that you've come to take for granted." -- John Michalski

### August: Do Not Panic

Bonsai trees often look their worst in August in New Jersey. This is completely normal:

- Foliage has hardened off; the big growth push of May and June is over
- Mature leaves may show sunburn, insect damage, or minor fungal spotting
- Deciduous species (maple, apple, beech, honeysuckle) are most affected
- Reduce watering only slightly; continue monitoring; do not defoliate or repot
- Trees will look dramatically better after fall leaf drop and again in spring

### Bonsai as Long-Term Practice

From GSBS newsletters: "What could be more optimistic than grooming a small tree with an eye to what it will look like a year from now, or five years from now? Each little snip and bend you put into your trees has a distinct goal based not on what you did this very minute, but on how the plant will mature and grow into the work you've put into it, to create a pleasing, natural-looking result in the weeks, months, and years ahead."

---

## Winter Protection: Advanced Details

### Temperature Thresholds for Winter Storage

Not all bonsai have the same cold tolerance. Two species commonly grown in NJ that need more protected winter storage:

- Japanese Black Pine: roots cannot survive a deep freeze. Store in an unheated shed or garage that stays above 35 degrees F.
- Trident Maple: same as above -- roots cannot survive deep freeze. Store above 35 degrees F.
- Most other NJ-hardy bonsai (juniper, larch, hornbeam, elm) tolerate colder storage as long as the soil does not repeatedly freeze and thaw.

### Glazed Pot Winter Storage Technique

Glazed pots are at risk of spalling and cracking during freeze-thaw cycles. Technique for wintering trees in glazed pots:

1. Lift the root mass carefully out of the glazed pot
2. Nest the bare root mass into a container of milled peat moss (acts as insulation and moisture buffer)
3. Store the glazed pot separately indoors or wrapped to protect from weather
4. Repot into the glazed pot in spring when repotting season begins

### Cold Frame Storage (Mark Schmuck Method)

GSBS member Mark Schmuck shared his cold frame approach for protecting precious bonsai:

- Trees are nestled pot-and-all into a bed of soft milled peat moss inside the cold frame
- Milled peat insulates pots against drastic temperature changes
- Peat also maintains even moisture around the pots during dormancy
- A raised bed with mortared walls works for accommodating multiple medium-sized trees

### Anti-Desiccant Spray (Wilt-Pruf)

Some GSBS members apply Wilt-Pruf anti-desiccant spray to evergreen foliage before winter:

- Helps trees retain healthy foliage through winter and into spring
- Particularly useful for Yew (Taxus), Azalea, and broadleaf evergreens
- The coating may develop a milky haze after several months in sun -- this is harmless and fades over time
- Apply on a dry day above freezing before trees go into winter storage

### Warm Spells During Winter Storage Warning

A commonly overlooked winter risk: unseasonably warm weather in late winter can cause dormant trees to break bud while still in storage. If a warm spell hits:

- Move stored trees outside or ventilate the cold frame or shed
- Bring them back inside before nighttime temperatures drop
- Failure to do this can result in spindly new growth that then freezes when cold returns
- Storage enclosures (sheds, garages, cold frames) can get considerably warmer than outdoor air temperature on sunny winter days

### Dehydration -- The Most Common Cause of Winter Bonsai Loss

"One of the most common causes of bonsai death is dehydration during the cold months." -- GSBS newsletter

- Before the first hard freeze, make sure all bonsai soil is evenly moist
- Check stored trees monthly throughout winter; water lightly if soil has become dry
- Leafless trees need much less water, but the soil must never completely dry out

---

## Advanced Bonsai Techniques (From GSBS Demonstrations)

### Thread Grafting

Thread grafting is used to add foliage in areas where a branch lacks ramification or where back-budding has not occurred. Demonstrated by GSBS Artist in Residence Sergio Cuan on Japanese maple:

- A small hole is drilled through the branch or trunk at the desired location
- A young flexible shoot is threaded through the hole so that foliage emerges on the desired side
- The shoot is wrapped and secured; once it fuses with the host branch, the original connection is severed
- Cambium alignment is critical for the graft to take
- This technique allows the artist to place foliage exactly where the design requires it

### Jin Implant (Adding Deadwood Where None Exists)

Demonstrated by GSBS member John Michalski on a dwarf Alberta spruce:

- A jin implant involves inserting a piece of carved deadwood into the live material of the tree
- This creates dramatic deadwood features on trees that have no natural deadwood
- The technique allows artists to add structural interest to trees without waiting decades for natural deadwood development
- The wood is carved and treated with lime sulfur to preserve and whiten it

### Grafting for Back-Budding Problems

When a collected tree (yamadori) refuses to produce foliage close to the trunk, grafting is used to place new shoots where needed. Demonstrated by GSBS Potter in Residence Fred Aufschlager on a collected Eastern Red Cedar:

- Select active growing tips as scions
- Cut scions with a clean diagonal cut at the base
- Cut a receiving notch on the branch where foliage is needed
- Insert the scion so its cambium lines up with the branch cambium
- Wrap tightly with plastic tape to secure and prevent drying
- Once the graft takes, foliage fills in the previously bare area

### Species-Specific Pinching: Amur Maple

Amur Maple requires a different pinching approach than most deciduous bonsai:

- Do NOT bud-pinch Amur Maple -- this causes dieback at the pinch point
- Instead, allow new shoots to extend and harden off fully
- Once shoots are hardened, cut back to the first or second set of leaves
- This produces better ramification and stronger new growth than bud-pinching

### Bush Honeysuckle Pinching

Bush honeysuckle (Lonicera) is one of the most rewarding species for developing fine ramification:

- Pinch the newest pair of leaves as soon as they are large enough to pinch
- This species responds to 5-6 pinching cycles per growing season
- Repeated pinching creates extremely fine, dense branching very quickly
- Do NOT cut with scissors -- pinch with fingertips

---

## Fertilizer Notes from GSBS Practitioners

### Osmocote for Bonsai

John Michalski's preferred fertilizer approach:

"I've placed my faith in pelleted surface fertilizer (Osmocote) that releases small amounts of the good stuff every time you water, or every time it rains. The products you mix in the watering can work fine too, except that they will leach out when it rains."

Application guidelines:
- Apply when buds begin to expand in spring
- Scatter pellets on the soil surface
- Continue through summer; stop around August as the focus shifts to hardening
- Exception: for refined trees (those being held at a fixed size), hold off on fertilizer until the first set of leaves has hardened off -- to avoid pushing excessive new growth

### The Fertilizer Debate

"Ask 20 bonsai practitioners about fertilizers, soil mixes, and repotting schedules, and you are liable to receive 20 different answers. Nowadays, with so many genuine bonsai masters making their expertise available via YouTube, you can get all kinds of good advice from the people with the most experience." -- GSBS newsletter

The consistent rule across all approaches: switch to low-nitrogen (high potassium and phosphorus) fertilizer by late summer, and stop all nitrogen by October.


---

## Cold-Hardy "Bulletproof" Species for New Jersey Bonsai

GSBS member John Michalski, after losing over half his collection to a combination of vole infestations, month-long August drought, severe freeze-thaw cycles, and heat waves, identified the species that proved truly bulletproof through all conditions in Northern New Jersey:

Proven NJ survivors: Amur Maple (Acer ginnala), Yew (Taxus cuspidata), Amur Honeysuckle (Lonicera morrowii), Arborvitae / Northern White Cedar (Thuja occidentalis), Common Apple (Malus pumila), Eastern Hemlock (Tsuga canadensis), Eastern Red Cedar (Juniperus virginiana), and American Larch (Larix laricina).

What made these species survive while others did not: they tolerate heat, drought, freezing, and physical root damage. Yew was noted as especially remarkable -- it "didn't seem to notice a thing" during a month-long summer drought that killed nearly every other species in the collection. Arborvitae and Hemlock, despite coming from moist forest environments, also "continued to flourish."

Species that proved fragile in NJ conditions (heat/drought sensitive): Black Spruce (dropped needles in heatwaves), American Beech, European Beech, American Larch (all died in 2005 drought), Black Birch, Red Maple, and American Bald Cypress.

---

## Dormancy Requirements for Hardy Outdoor Bonsai

Hardy temperate bonsai require a genuine cold dormancy period to survive long-term. From GSBS newsletters:

"Your temperate bonsai require a sustained cold spell at or below 40 degrees Fahrenheit (5 degrees Celsius) for a period of about 6-8 weeks. Once local temperatures fall consistently below this number, your trees will go into full dormancy."

"Exposing them to higher temperatures for more than a few hours may cause them to break dormancy and begin growing which, in January-March, is likely to result in the death of the tree, or at least a serious weakening."

"If you need to bring trees indoors to work on them during winter, only for a few hours, and the colder the workspace, the better. Put on a coat and work on them outdoors if possible."

Sunlight during dormancy: "Your temperate bonsai do not require sunlight during dormancy, so keep them out of the sun. No need to worry about photosynthesis between November and early March, even for evergreen bonsai." -- GSBS newsletter, John Michalski

---

## Winter Storage Methods: A Full Comparison

GSBS members have experimented with every practical winter storage method for Northern New Jersey.

### Method 1: Ground Planting (Best for Small Collections)

Plant trees pot-and-all directly into the ground in a shaded location. A northern exposure where the sun will not reach the pots during winter months is ideal. Some experienced members remove trees from their containers entirely and plant directly into loose soil for even better temperature buffering. Mulch lightly over the soil surface.

Important: locate trees where roof runoff (rain or snow sliding off a sloped roof) will not pour directly on them.

### Method 2: Densely-Packed Cluster Against a Wind-Break

John Michalski's preferred method since 2010, after voles devastated his cold frame: place entire collection tightly packed together along the shady side of a privacy fence or wall on the southwest side of property, without any cover. Trees frozen solid in February through late March, but protected from wind. "I see to it that they are watered in mild weather and the pots snowcovered whenever the weather provides."

### Method 3: Cold Frame

A small shed with a removable roof, often sunk about one foot into the ground. Can be purchased ready-made or built. Risk: "These enclosures can remain surprisingly warm, causing trees to pop new buds or refuse to harden off." Ventilate on warm winter days.

### Method 4: Hoop House (DIY)

A practical and inexpensive option for larger collections. Construction from GSBS newsletter:
- Use flexible PVC piping in 10-foot lengths
- Pound 1-foot lengths of 3/8-inch iron rebar half-way into the ground at intervals
- Bend PVC over the rebar to form hoops
- Cover the frame with a large sheet of light-colored tarp or plastic
- Do NOT use clear plastic -- it will act as a greenhouse and overheat

### Method 5: Unheated Shed or Garage

Works well, but watch the temperature. "Enclosed spaces can get much warmer on a sunny winter day than you might expect. If they don't get much warmer than 40 degrees you're all right." Move trees outside briefly during unexpected warm spells to prevent premature bud break.

---

## Pottery and Winter: High-Fired vs. Low-Fired Containers

This is one of the most practically important distinctions for NJ bonsai growers:

- High-fired bonsai pottery (true stoneware): does not absorb moisture. Safe for outdoor wintering in New Jersey.
- Glazed pottery: retains moisture under the external glaze, which expands when frozen. Risk of spalling (fracturing, flaking, breaking into shards). Remove trees from glazed pots before winter or store glazed pots indoors.
- Low-fired pottery and terra cotta: absorbs moisture that expands as it freezes. Will crack and break. Do not use for outdoor bonsai in NJ winters.

Technique for wintering in glazed pots: carefully lift the root mass out of the glazed pot, plant root mass directly into ground or nestle into milled peat moss, and store the pot separately indoors.

---

## Using Snow as Free Irrigation

From GSBS newsletter: "Any time there is snowfall, it is a good idea to blanket the soil surface of your bonsai with snow if that hasn't occurred naturally -- any time ambient temperatures rise above freezing, your trees will receive a little water." -- John Michalski

"Snow is good as an insulator, and whenever temperatures rise above freezing, the melting snow will water your bonsai for you -- whenever it snows I make sure to scatter some on top of the soil of my bonsai pots, if it hasn't reached them naturally."

Caution: beware of heavy snowfall and ice, which can break branches or damage buds. Shake snow off branches if accumulation is significant.

---

## The Magnolia Frost Indicator

For knowing when spring frosts are truly over in Northern New Jersey, GSBS member John Michalski offers a reliable natural indicator:

"I never consider my garden safe until the Magnolia blossoms have dropped to the ground without being turned brown by a late freeze."

Magnolia petals are highly susceptible to frost -- a late freeze quickly turns them a muddy chocolate brown. If the Magnolia blooms fully and the petals drop naturally (pale pink, not brown), the frost season has genuinely passed. Once this happens, it is safe to move tender material outdoors and resume feeding in earnest.

---

## Phoenix Graft and Approach Graft Techniques

### Phoenix Graft

The Phoenix Graft is an advanced deadwood technique demonstrated at GSBS by member John Michalski:

Young, flexible saplings are attached and encouraged to grow along the outside of an attractive piece of deadwood. Over time, the young plant bonds to the deadwood, producing the appearance of a massive ancient dead trunk with narrow veins of living tissue winding over and around it. The effect is dramatically aged and can transform ordinary deadwood into spectacular bonsai material.

Example from GSBS: Bill Muldowney's Shimpaku Juniper -- "provides an excellent example of this technique applied very successfully."

### Approach Graft

An approach graft attaches a shoot from one tree onto the trunk or large branch of another tree of the same species. The same technique can be done using a long shoot from the same tree. Useful when a collected tree has excellent character but a bare spot on the trunk where a branch is needed. The grafted shoot eventually fuses to the host, filling the gap in the design. Demonstrated on Eastern Red Cedar collected from railroad tracks in Morris County, NJ.

---

## Raffia Technique for Bending Heavy Branches

When bending thick or mature branches -- particularly on collected pines -- wrapping the branch in raffia before bending prevents the wood from splitting:

1. Moisten natural raffia until pliable
2. Wrap tightly around the section of branch to be bent, overlapping by about half the width of each wrap
3. Apply wire over the raffia
4. Bend slowly and gradually -- never force it in one movement
5. Leave raffia in place until the branch has set (typically one full growing season)

Demonstrated by GSBS members assisting a Japanese guest artist (Koji Hiramatsu) on a wild-collected Pitch Pine at GSBS, October 2018.

---

## Air-Layering: Step-by-Step Timeline for New Jersey

From GSBS newsletter, John Michalski's Japanese Maple air-layer documented month by month:

May (start): Once the crown of leaves has developed and hardened off, remove a 1-inch ring of bark and cambium from the point just below where you want the new root crown to develop. Take a small plastic nursery pot, slice it open vertically along one side. Position the pot around the surgery, fill with medium-grade bonsai mix, and tape the seam closed. Move tree to partial shade. Keep the soil in the small pot consistently moist -- never let it dry out.

July (2 months): Small roots are visible projecting from the drainage holes of the small pot. Good sign; continue as before.

Early September (4 months): Well-developed root system with "well-formed surface roots (nebari) completely encircle the area at the upper edge of where bark and cambium had been removed." This is the last opportunity to pot up air-layers -- waiting longer risks roots being too undeveloped to survive winter.

October: Carefully saw the layered portion from the base of the tree. Open the small pot to reveal the root system. Examine the roots and determine the best planting angle. Plant in a regular bonsai pot with well-draining mix.

Winter after potting: The new roots have not established in their container. "Could easily be killed by dehydration, or repeated freezing and thawing." Give this tree extra-careful winter protection -- warmer than other trees, well-moistened before hard freezes.

---

## Marking Trees During Bloom for Later Collection

A practical tip from GSBS newsletters for identifying collectible wild trees:

Many desirable species -- bush honeysuckle, wild apple, crabapple, quince -- are at their most identifiable and most interesting during flowering season (late April to early May for most). Desirable flowering forms (rare pink-flowered honeysuckle vs. common white; ornamental crabapples) are easiest to select when in bloom.

"Tie brilliant plastic tree-marking tape near the base of the more desirable plants, so that you can find them next spring, when the season is more appropriate for collection." -- John Michalski

The tape survives through the season and can be spotted again in March-April when collecting is safe and appropriate.

---

## Initial Design: The "Cleaning" Process (Fred Aufschlager Method)

When beginning work on raw bonsai material (nursery or collected), GSBS Potter in Residence Fred Aufschlager recommends this systematic approach:

1. Cleaning the tree: remove all dead, unhealthy, unproductive, and heavily shaded foliage. Remove extraneous branches and trunks that will never be part of the tree's future design.

2. Make a jin of every removed branch immediately -- do not put this off until "later." Collect the small stub, remove the bark, and apply lime sulfur. This prevents dead wood from rotting and adds character from day one.

3. Start or enhance a shari (deadwood area on the trunk) by connecting existing dead branches on conifers. Even a small shari from the beginning establishes the tree's age and character.

4. Wire principal remaining branches for initial placement.

Aufschlager's key insight: "Save Yourself YEARS of development time -- Start with Good Material." A slightly larger, more advanced nursery specimen ($40) vs. the smallest available material ($13-18) may represent years of saved development time and added enjoyment -- the extra $20-25 is almost always worth it.
